claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

First of all, could you please tell me how many children you'd like to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who is 16 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Since Alex is 16 years old, their eligibility depends on what they're currently doing. 

Could you please tell me if Alex is currently in any of the following:
- Full-time education (such as A-levels, T-levels, or equivalent)
- Approved training (such as a traineeship)
- Or has Alex left education/training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex has recently left education. They've just signed up with the local careers service to figure out what they want to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know.

That's helpful information. When a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service, you may be able to continue receiving Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks.

Just a couple more questions to confirm your eligibility:

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Just a few more questions to confirm eligibility:

Does Alex live with you, or do you contribute towards their upkeep (for example, by paying for food, clothes, or pocket money)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. And one final question:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. I now have everything I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's a summary of why:

- ✅ You live in the UK
- ✅ Alex lives with you, so you are responsible for them
- ✅ Although Alex is 16 and has left education, they have registered with a government-sponsored careers service
- ✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

**Important to note:** Because Alex has left education and registered with a careers service, your Child Benefit can continue for **up to 20 weeks**. After this period, eligibility will depend on whether Alex returns to approved education or training, or their circumstances change.

If Alex decides to go back into approved education or training (such as college or a traineeship), you may continue to be eligible for Child Benefit until they turn 20.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and has recently left education but has registered with a government-sponsored careers service. According to the Child Benefit rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Additionally, the claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), Alex lives with them (making them responsible for the child), and no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. They live in the UK, are responsible for Alex (who lives with them), and Alex qualifies as an eligible child because, although they are 16 and have left education, they have registered with a government-sponsored careers service. This allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.
